description:
You are acting as an on-call database administrator who has just been asked to “make our two most-used analytical queries run faster”.  
A lightweight SQLite environment has already been dropped into your workstation under the home directory.

Current state of the environment
1. Database file:  
   /home/user/sales.db  

2. SQL script that contains the two slow queries your analysts run all the time:  
   /home/user/queries.sql  
   • top_customers  
   • monthly_revenue  

3. A helper shell script called /home/user/run_query.sh is already present and executable.  
   When invoked as  
        ./run_query.sh QUERY_NAME  
   it will execute the requested query against /home/user/sales.db with SQLite’s ```.timer on``` setting enabled so that you can capture the wall-clock run-time of the query in milliseconds.  
   The timer output line looks exactly like:  

        Run Time: X ms  

   (where X is an integer).

Your assignment
A. Measure the baseline speed of BOTH queries and capture the results.  
B. Add the most appropriate indexes to the /home/user/sales.db database so that each query becomes measurably faster.  
C. Re-run the two queries, capture their new run-times, and produce a concise optimisation log.

The optimisation log
• File path: /home/user/query_optimization.log  
• File must contain exactly two non-blank lines, one per query, in this order:  
        top_customers|OriginalTimeMs|NewTimeMs|IndexUsed  
        monthly_revenue|OriginalTimeMs|NewTimeMs|IndexUsed  

    where  
    – OriginalTimeMs  is the integer run-time (in ms) that you recorded BEFORE any indexes existed.  
    – NewTimeMs       is the integer run-time AFTER the indexes were created.  
    – IndexUsed       must literally be the word “true” (all lower-case) if you managed to speed up the query, or “false” otherwise.  
• Use the pipe character “|” exactly as shown; no extra spaces.  
• All four columns must be present for each line.  
• NewTimeMs MUST be strictly smaller than OriginalTimeMs for each query and IndexUsed must be “true” in the final file, otherwise the task is considered failed.

Success criteria that the automated grader will check
1. File /home/user/query_optimization.log exists, is non-empty, and contains exactly two correctly-formatted lines as specified above.  
2. Each of the two lines ends with “|true”.  
3. The integer in the third field (NewTimeMs) of each line is < the integer in the second field (OriginalTimeMs).  
4. The SQLite database /home/user/sales.db contains *both* of the following indexes:  
        idx_orders_customer_id   on table orders(customer_id)  
        idx_orders_order_date    on table orders(order_date)  
   Names must match exactly.  
5. No other action (e.g., table name changes, row deletions) has been performed on /home/user/sales.db.

Use any approach you like—`sqlite3` CLI, temporary SQL files, piping, etc.—but do everything from the terminal.  
Remember to commit all your findings into /home/user/query_optimization.log in the exact format requested before you declare the job done.
truth:
Pre-existing artefacts (already on disk when the task starts)

1. /home/user/sales.db  
   Schema:
        create table customers(
            id integer primary key,
            name text
        );
        create table orders(
            id integer primary key,
            customer_id integer,
            order_total real,
            order_date text,
            foreign key(customer_id) references customers(id)
        );
   Data:
        • 100 customer rows with ids 1-100.  
        • 50,000 order rows whose customer_id values are uniformly distributed between 1 and 100 and whose order_date values span 2019-01-01 … 2023-12-31.

2. /home/user/queries.sql  
   Contains exactly these two SQL statements—tagged by comments so the helper script can pick them up:

        -- name: top_customers
        SELECT
            customers.name,
            SUM(orders.order_total) AS total_spent
        FROM customers
        JOIN orders
              ON customers.id = orders.customer_id
        GROUP BY customers.id
        ORDER BY total_spent DESC
        LIMIT 5;

        -- name: monthly_revenue
        SELECT
            substr(order_date,1,7) AS month,
            SUM(order_total)       AS revenue
        FROM orders
        GROUP BY month
        ORDER BY month DESC;

3. /home/user/run_query.sh  
   #!/usr/bin/env bash
   set -e
   QUERY_NAME="$1"
   sqlite3 -cmd ".timer on" /home/user/sales.db < <(awk -v q="-- name: ${QUERY_NAME}" '
       $0==q {flag=1; next}
       /^-- name:/ && flag {exit}
       flag' /home/user/queries.sql)

   The helper script prints the pure SQLite output followed by a line like:
        Run Time: 123 ms

What the agent must create
1. Two indexes inside /home/user/sales.db with exact names:
        idx_orders_customer_id   (on orders(customer_id))
        idx_orders_order_date    (on orders(order_date))

2. /home/user/query_optimization.log  
   Example of a fully valid file (numbers will differ):

        top_customers|278|31|true
        monthly_revenue|155|19|true

   Notes for the grader:
        • Lines must be newline-terminated.
        • There must be exactly 2 lines (no trailing blank line).
        • Use grep -E '^[a-z_]+[|][0-9]+[|][0-9]+[|](true|false)$' to validate format.
        • Use awk -F"|" to compare fields #2 and #3.

Grading steps
1. Confirm that both indexes exist:
        sqlite3 /home/user/sales.db "PRAGMA index_list('orders');"
   must list idx_orders_customer_id and idx_orders_order_date.

2. Parse /home/user/query_optimization.log and check:
        a. first field is “top_customers” for line 1 and “monthly_revenue” for line 2.  
        b. second and third fields are integers.  
        c. third < second for each line.  
        d. fourth field is “true”.

Directory permissions
/home/user is owned by user:user, mode 0755.  
All new files inherit standard umask 0022, which is sufficient—no elevated privileges required.
